Applicable RBA cash rate target on publication date: 1.0%

Article text:
The company backed by some of the world's biggest private equity investors and speculated to be one of Australia's biggest technology listings of the year, PropertyGuru, is eyeing new markets for its real estate classifieds business.

PropertyGuru chief executive Hari Krishnan told The Australian Financial Review that the south-east Asia property classifieds and services business has a front-row seat to monitor real estate development in the region, and is looking at opportunities to enter new markets, as it did in Vietnam.

But it's only going to leap into markets where it can become the number one player, he said.

"We're not interested in planting flags. We're in five markets, there's a big addressable market in just these five," Mr Krishnan said.

"Instead of just spreading and proliferating, we're much more interested in going deep, bringing these digital solutions; we're not just a marketplace. I think it's much more about can you get to that [market leading] position, monetise, exercise pricing power, build a strong financial profile in that market. We're seeing that already in Singapore, we're seeing that very much so in Vietnam, and increasingly so in the other markets."

In the 19th century sterling was king, in the 20th the greenback; now the yuan is in the ascendancy, writes Angus Grigg.

Each weekday morning for the past five years China's central bank made an announcement few ever noticed. At 9.15 am from its Soviet style headquarters in Beijing, the People's Bank of China released the "daily fix" - the rate at which the yuan will trade against the US dollar.

Even for most committed market watchers it was hard going, worthy only of reporting as a matter of record as the currency was tediously stable.

Then on Tuesday everything changed.

Without notice or explanation the PBOC lowered the "daily fix" by 1.9 per cent against the US dollar, the largest devaluation in 20 years. Investors responded by hitting the panic button. Boyne Smelters general manager Joe Rea said the cuts were "heart wrenching" and "a little soul destroying" after the workforce had toiled to improve productivity and safety achieved over the past year.

The 585,000 tonne a year plant is cutting production by 14 per cent and an equivalent cut from the 1000-strong workforce would take out more than 100 jobs.

It must have spoken to a need, and it's still speaking to a need. It's a top-selling self-help title, with 30 million sales and counting. It has spawned copycats and spoofs, like the amusingly named memoir-turned-film, How To Lose Friends And Alienate People.
